skill.name: zarr-python
skill.description: Guidance for using Zarr Python to store large chunked arrays with compression, metadata, cloud storage, and NumPy, Dask, or Xarray workflows.
skill.summary: Build efficient chunked array storage workflows with Zarr Python.
skill.author: K-Dense-AI

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high and critical patterns, but review found them to be documentation false positives. Markdown backticks, compression codec names, ordinary data keys, and reference URLs do not show code execution, weak cryptography, credential theft, or reconnaissance. The remaining risk is low because the skill includes package installation guidance and cloud storage examples that may use user-managed credentials.

low_findings.title: Package Installation Guidance
low_findings.description: The skill shows uv pip install commands for zarr, s3fs, and gcsfs. This is legitimate setup guidance, but users should review packages before installation.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are explicit package installation examples in documentation. They are not executed by the skill, so the residual risk is user-controlled dependency installation.

low_findings.title: Credentialed Cloud Storage Examples
low_findings.description: The skill demonstrates S3 and Google Cloud Storage access, including an S3 example that uses credentials. This is expected for Zarr cloud workflows but needs normal credential hygiene.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The examples clearly use cloud storage libraries and user-controlled bucket paths. No exfiltration endpoint or secret collection behavior was found.

low_findings.title: External Documentation Links
low_findings.description: The skill links to Zarr, Xarray, Dask, NumCodecs, GitHub, and community documentation. These are ordinary reference links, not network calls made by the skill.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The lines are a resource list of public project documentation URLs. There is no code that sends data to these destinations.

dangerous_patterns.title: Static Backtick Execution Findings Dismissed
dangerous_patterns.description: The external command findings are Markdown code spans and fenced examples, not Ruby or shell backtick execution. No executable skill script was found.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The cited files are Markdown documentation. Backticks delimit code examples and inline API names rather than executing commands.

dangerous_patterns.title: Static Weak Cryptography Findings Dismissed
dangerous_patterns.description: The weak cryptography hits refer to Zarr descriptions, compression codecs, or version text. They are not cryptographic operations or password handling.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: Semantic review shows the suspicious terms occur in array storage documentation. No encryption, hashing, certificate, or authentication implementation appears at those locations.

dangerous_patterns.title: Static Reconnaissance Findings Dismissed
dangerous_patterns.description: The reconnaissance hits are guidance about chunk sizing and anti-patterns for array storage. No host discovery, port scanning, or environment probing was found.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines discuss memory, chunk sizes, and storage workflow cautions. They do not request system inventory or network discovery.

dangerous_patterns.title: Static Certificate Key Finding Dismissed
dangerous_patterns.description: The sensitive file hit is a reference to Zarr group keys in an example that lists array contents. It is not a certificate or private key file.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The word keys is used as a data structure method in a Zarr group example. No credential material or key file path is present.

content.value_statement: Large scientific arrays are hard to store, chunk, and share efficiently. This skill helps Claude, Codex, and Claude Code design Zarr Python workflows for local and cloud storage.
actual_capabilities: Explains how to create, open, read, write, resize, and append Zarr arrays.
Guides chunk size choices based on memory, compression, and access patterns.
Shows local, memory, ZIP, S3, and Google Cloud Storage store patterns.
Covers array groups, attributes, metadata consolidation, and hierarchy inspection.
Connects Zarr workflows with NumPy, Dask, Xarray, and compression codecs.
Describes common performance tuning and concurrent write practices.
limitations: It provides guidance and examples, not a packaged Zarr application.
Cloud examples require separate credentials, permissions, and storage libraries.
It does not validate real datasets or benchmark a user environment automatically.
API details may need checking against the installed Zarr Python version.

use_cases.title: Plan climate data storage
use_cases.description: Design chunked time, latitude, and longitude arrays for efficient daily reads and appends.

use_cases.title: Move arrays to object storage
use_cases.description: Create S3 or Google Cloud Storage layouts with metadata consolidation and practical chunk sizing.

use_cases.title: Integrate array tools
use_cases.description: Connect Zarr stores with NumPy, Dask, and Xarray while avoiding full-array memory loads.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Basic Store
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner setup for a local array
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me create a local Zarr array for a 10000 by 10000 float32 dataset. Recommend chunk shape and compression.

prompt_templates.title: Choose Chunking Strategy
prompt_templates.scenario: Tune storage for a known access pattern
prompt_templates.prompt: I read one month of daily climate grids at a time. Recommend Zarr chunks for time, latitude, and longitude dimensions.

prompt_templates.title: Design Cloud Storage
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a Zarr workflow for S3 storage with consolidated metadata, credential safety notes, and efficient reads from multiple machines.

prompt_templates.title: Optimize Dask and Xarray
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my planned Zarr, Dask, and Xarray workflow for a multi-terabyte dataset. Identify chunking, metadata, and concurrency risks.

output_examples.input: I need to store daily temperature grids for one year.
output: Recommended layout: one time chunk per day with full spatial tiles when daily reads dominate.
Use compression tuned for float data and keep each chunk near a few megabytes.
Store descriptive units, calendar, and processing version in array attributes.

output_examples.input: I want to publish a Zarr dataset in cloud storage.
output: Use object storage paths controlled by your project account.
Consolidate metadata before broad read access to reduce listing overhead.
Keep credentials outside notebooks and avoid embedding secrets in shared examples.

output_examples.input: My Dask workflow loads too much data into memory.
output: Align Dask chunks with Zarr chunks when possible.
Avoid whole-array reads for summary statistics.
Compute over chunked slices and persist only intermediate results that justify memory cost.

best_practices: Choose chunks around the expected read pattern before optimizing compression.
Use consolidated metadata for cloud stores with many arrays or chunks.
Keep cloud credentials in standard provider configuration, not in prompts or notebooks.
anti_patterns: Do not load an entire large Zarr array when slice or chunk reads are enough.
Do not choose tiny chunks that create excessive metadata and object requests.
Do not let multiple writers update the same chunks without synchronization.

faq.question: What does this skill help with?
faq.answer: It helps design Zarr Python workflows for chunked arrays, compression, metadata, and cloud storage.

faq.question: Does it execute Zarr code automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides guidance and examples that a user can adapt and run in their environment.

faq.question: Can it help with S3 or Google Cloud Storage?
faq.answer: Yes. It explains storage patterns, metadata consolidation, and credential hygiene for cloud object stores.

faq.question: Which Python tools does it connect with?
faq.answer: It covers common workflows with NumPy, Dask, Xarray, s3fs, gcsfs, and NumCodecs.

faq.question: How should I choose chunk sizes?
faq.answer: Start from the main read pattern, then balance memory limits, compression, and cloud request overhead.

faq.question: Is it suitable for production pipelines?
faq.answer: It can support planning, but production pipelines still need testing, permissions review, and version checks.
